We hope this resource will facilitate research and hypothesis generation in a manner that is accessible to both experts and non-experts in data analysis. Chapter 2 Using HumanIslets.com 2.1 Omics 2.2 Donor & Phenotype 2.3 Search & Discovery 2.4 Data Download

Chapter 2 Using HumanIslets.com The platform’s left-hand navigation groups features into four blocks — Omics, Donor & Phenotype, Search & Discovery, and Data Download. This chapter is organized in the same order, with one section per page in the sidebar. 2.1 Omics The Omics block holds the analyses that operate primarily on feature-level molecular data: differential-expression-style omics analysis, multi-omics integration, and the genome-scale flux atlas. 2.1.1 Omics View — individual omics analysis HumanIslet.com presently includes information from 626 human research islet isolations that includes donor and technical metadata, islet and islet cell function, and omics. Omics datasets include bulk proteomics, bulk RNAseq, gene panels (Nanostring), patch-seq, and pseudobulk RNAseq for alpha- and beta-cells. Omics-metadata relationships can be analysed by: Selecting the ‘Omics Type’ of interest Selecting the ‘Primary Metadata’ to be analysed, and ‘Comparison of Interest’ if applicable Choosing covariates to control for Many covariates are available for correction and selection may depend on comparisons being made or the objective of the user. Note however we have reported important impacts of correcting for ‘culture time’, particularly when examining RNAseq data, and ‘Non-endocrine cell proportion (computed)’ when comparing groups with dramatically different preparation purities. Selecting donors to include Choosing ‘Subset’ will open a window that allows for filtering by donor characteristics and data availability, or upload of a list of donor IDs or RRIDs of interest Selecting a p-value cutoff, and the use of adjusted p-values Click ‘submit’ The number of donors included in the analysis will be displayed. If too few donors are available for the chosen analysis (<10), an error message will be shown. Results of the analysis will be shown under ‘Feature Association Results’, and both results and analysis pipeline can be downloaded by clicking on the appropriate button. Results are shown as a graphical representation (volcano plot) or table, accessible by clicking on the appropriate tab. Clicking on features (points in the volcano plot, or table entries) will raise a graph showing categorical or scatterplot representations of the data/feature of interest. Clicking on individual donors within those plots will take you to the ‘Donor View’ page for that donor, allowing for inspection of potential outlier donors. In the Results Table, clicking on the feature name will take you to the ‘Feature Search’ page for that gene/protein. At the right are links to the NCBI gene page and T2D Knowledge Portal (T2DKP) entries for each feature (external sites), to a single-cell RNAseq reference dataset (SC) to inspect cell-type specific expression of the feature, and to the platform’s own Knowledge Search (KG) page (section 2.3.1) for an integrated multi-source view of the feature. The third tab under the ‘Feature Association Results’ pulls up a Pathway Analysis tool. The enrichment method offered depends on the omics type, because gene-based, metabolite-based, and reaction-based feature lists each require matched annotation libraries: Gene-based omics (RNA-seq, Nanostring, proteomics, pseudobulk, patch-seq) — Overrepresentation Analysis (ORA) and Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) against KEGG, Reactome, GO, and the GEM gene libraries. Metabolomics — MSEA (Metabolite Set Enrichment Analysis), the metabolomics analogue of GSEA, implemented via xialab’s MetaboAnalyst and ranking metabolites against curated metabolite sets (KEGG human metabolite-centred pathways and the GEM metabolite groupings). Metabolic flux — RSEA (Reaction Set Enrichment Analysis), ranking reactions against Human-GEM subsystem groupings (glycolysis, TCA cycle, fatty-acid metabolism, etc.). This is the analogue of GSEA at the reaction level. To perform pathway / metabolite / reaction analysis: Select ORA or GSEA / MSEA / RSEA as appropriate for the omics type ORA is performed on significant hits based on the p-value threshold set above GSEA / MSEA / RSEA can be performed using log2FC/coefficient or T-statistic for ranking, and can be performed even in cases when no individual features are marked as significant Select whether to collapse redundant pathways / sets Choose a false discovery rate (FDR) cutoff Choose among the available libraries for the matched omics type Click ‘submit’ The ‘Pathway Results’ will be displayed in both graphical and table format, selected by clicking on the appropriate tab. As above, both results and analysis pipeline can be downloaded by clicking on the appropriate button. In the interactive graphical view, significant pathways (based on FDR cutoff) are shown as ridgeplots. Clicking on these will raise heatmaps showing the contribution of individual genes/proteins. 2.1.2 Multi-Omics Analysis The Multi-Omics Analysis page lets users explore integrated analyses across two or three omics layers selected from a single dropdown (combinations are pre-computed; eligibility requires at least 20 donors with measurements in every selected omics). Three complementary methods are available, each answering a slightly different question and unlocked by selecting it from the Method dropdown: WGCNA (Weighted Gene Co-expression Network Analysis) is unsupervised. It groups features that move together across donors into modules, then summarises each module by a single representative value (its eigengene) so that module-level signal can be compared across omics and against the platform’s phenotype variables. Use WGCNA when the question is which features behave like a co-regulated programme, and does any module track a phenotype? MOFA (Multi-Omics Factor Analysis) is also unsupervised, but rather than discrete modules it learns continuous latent factors — directions of variation that several omics share. The same factor often explains a substantial chunk of variance in two or more views, which makes MOFA a good fit when the goal is to find the shared axes of variation across omics types. Donors with only partial omics coverage are still usable thanks to MOFA’s NA-tolerant fit. DIABLO (Data Integration Analysis for Biomarker discovery using Latent cOmponents) is supervised: it requires a primary metadata variable as the target and finds the smallest set of correlated features across omics that best discriminates two groups (or predicts a continuous outcome). Use DIABLO when the question is phrased as which combined feature signature best separates these donor groups? A help (?) tooltip next to the dropdown summarises these three methods inline. Full methodology and parameter choices are in chapter 5; here we walk through how to use each method on the page. To run a multi-omics analysis: Pick the Method (WGCNA, MOFA, or DIABLO) from the dropdown Choose Omics Type I and Omics Type II (and optionally Omics Type III) — the combinations are precomputed, so the choices that have ≥ 20 shared donors will be selectable For patch-seq, an extra cell type selector appears (α / β / δ / PP) For metabolite and flux, an extra glucose condition selector appears (low / high / ratio) For DIABLO only, pick a Primary Metadata variable and (for binary outcomes) the two contrast levels. WGCNA and MOFA are unsupervised and skip this step. Click Submit The result tabs (described below per method) then become available. Because every eligible combination is precomputed on the server, Submit returns almost instantly. WGCNA results layout. Four tabs: Overview — a heatmap with modules on the rows and core phenotypes on the columns. Each cell shows the module–phenotype association: Pearson r (signed, −1 to +1) for continuous and binary phenotypes, or ANOVA-derived effect size (unsigned, 0 to 1) for multi-level categoricals. Cells carry significance asterisks (* padj<0.05, ** <0.01, *** <0.001), and hovering any cell reveals the full r / p / padj / n. A Select Phenotypes button opens a dialog to choose which phenotypes appear on the columns (with a Reset to defaults shortcut), and the heatmap can be exported as PNG (image) or CSV (underlying matrix). This is the entry-point view for “which module tracks which phenotype, at a glance”. Trait Associations — drill-down view for a single phenotype chosen from the Group / Phenotype selectors; shows that phenotype’s association across every module in the combination (not just the curated “core” phenotype set in the Overview). Features — per-module feature table with module membership ranking and omics-type tags. Network — interactive node/edge view of the top topological-overlap edges, with nodes coloured by omics type and modules. MOFA results layout. Six tabs: Overview — a heatmap with MOFA factors on the rows and core phenotypes on the columns, using the same conventions as the WGCNA Overview: Pearson r for continuous/binary, effect size for multi-level categoricals, the same asterisk-based significance markers, the same hover detail, the same Select Phenotypes / Reset / PNG / CSV controls. This is the place to start when asking “which MOFA factor tracks which phenotype”. Variance Decomposition — per-factor per-view variance heatmap. Trait Associations — drill-down view for a single phenotype across every MOFA factor (analogue of WGCNA’s Trait Associations tab). Feature Loadings — top-weighted features per factor per omics. Feature Network — feature-level co-loading network across omics. Factor Space — donors plotted in factor space, with metadata-colouring options. DIABLO results layout. Three tabs (only populated when an analysis variable is selected): Significant Features — features selected by DIABLO across the selected omics, with their loadings and per-omics ranking. Correlation Network — cross-omics feature correlation network for the selected features. Pathway Analysis — ORA / GSEA on the DIABLO-selected feature list against the standard pathway libraries. All tables are downloadable as CSV from the corresponding tab. 2.1.3 Metabolic Atlas (should be Hide?) The Metabolic Atlas is the platform’s interactive view of human islet metabolism. Users browse the metabolic network reaction by reaction, overlay HumanIslets data (predicted flux, gene or protein expression, scRNA-seq, etc.) on top of it, and ask comparative questions such as how does this pathway differ between donor groups, which reactions are tied to this gene, or where in metabolism does this metabolite sit. Behind the scenes the network is the Human-GEM v1.19.0 genome-scale metabolic reconstruction; the underlying flux methodology is documented in chapter 4 (Metabolic Flux). When the page loads, the metabolic network appears in the centre of the screen, with the Pathways legend on the left listing the colour-coded pathway categories (and, when expanded, each individual pathway by name) — the legend is informational, used as a colour key for the network. The network itself can be panned and zoomed; clicking any edge opens a small detail dialog with the reaction(s) on that edge and their flux value, and clicking any node opens the Pathway Detail dialog for the pathway that node sits on. The Pathway Detail dialog is a draggable, resizable window that renders the selected pathway’s reaction–metabolite graph in full. When an enrichment overlay is in effect (for example after adding a flux or metabolomics layer), the dialog shows a coloured legend at the top: in metabolite-set mode, red nodes are increased, blue are decreased, and orange are not in the analysis set, with node size and colour intensity proportional to enrichment magnitude; reaction-set mode uses gradient bars rather than dot colours for the same direction encoding. A toolbar above the graph provides view controls (pan mode toggle, selection-area mode, Stretch / Compress edges for a selected region, Reset Pathway, Save Layout), labelling toggles (Show / Hide cofactor labels, Show / Hide metabolite labels, Show / Hide control nodes and control points), an Add / Delete Flux control for manual annotation, and PNG / SVG export buttons. A right-side Node Settings panel adjusts node size and colour, and an Edge Settings panel adjusts edge width and colour. To overlay data on the network: Click Add Layers in the top toolbar to open the Add Data Layers dialog Pick a Layer Type and an Omics Type — the dialog then surfaces the additional fields that match the chosen omics: Patch-seq (scRNA-seq) — choose cell type and glucose concentration Flux — choose glucose condition (low / high / ratio) Donor mode — type or pick a donor ID Cluster mode — pick a VAE cluster Comparison mode — pick a primary metadata variable, the two contrast levels (class-1 / class-2), covariates to control for, the donor scope (all donors or a subset), and a p-value cutoff with optional FDR adjustment Click Add Layer to apply it to the network. Multiple layers can be added in succession to compare conditions side by side. Click any node on the network to drill down — this opens the Pathway Detail dialog (described below) for that node’s pathway, with the active layer’s enrichment overlaid on the reactions and metabolites inside the pathway, so users can inspect where inside the pathway the signal is coming from. (The Pathways legend on the left is informational — used to colour-code categories — and is not itself clickable.) Once a layer is active, a second legend appears below the Pathways legend naming the active layer and showing the flux gradient bar (min / 0 / max), up- and down-regulation swatches, the Signal strength (node size) indicator (weak → strong), and a short summary listing how many pathways are matched plus the top up- and down-regulated pathways for the layer. The rest of the toolbar covers the common controls: Reset View restores the default layout, and the small menu toggle exposes Show / Hide Compartments, Show / Hide Legend, Download Plot (exports the current view as an image), and Download Pathway Results (downloads the layer’s per-pathway statistics, enabled once at least one layer is added). 2.2 Donor & Phenotype The Donor & Phenotype block groups the views that browse the cohort at the donor level: per-donor profiles, phenotype-only exploration, and multi-omics-derived donor stratification. 2.2.1 Donor View The Donor View page allows examination of individual donor, islet isolation, and functional data from each of 626 donors currently in the HumanIslets.com database. Donors can be searched by ADI IsletCore Donor ID or Research Resource ID (RRID). Data categories can be selected from the left-hand menu, organized into four groups: Metadata (Donor Information, Isolation Information); Genetic Variables (Ancestry, Genetic Risk Score); Insulin Secretion (Static GSIS plus separate Dynamic Insulin Response views for Glucose, Leucine, and Palmitate); and Other (Prohormone Expression, Mitochondrial Function, Single-cell electrophysiology, Pancreas Lipid Extraction, and Omics Availability). These metadata are described in detail in chapters 3 and 4, below. Typically, data is shown for selected donor in the context of the entire dataset, allowing for easy inspection and determination of whether a donor falls within the normal range of the population. A few points that are worth noting here: Donor Information shows the RRID if available. Clicking on this will link out to the NCBI Biosample page for this donor Isolation Information also contains the current inventory of snap frozen and cryopreserved islet tubes for that donor (all donors also have paraffin embedded biopsies) Isolation Information includes cell type composition calculated by deconvolution in donors with bulk proteomics data (see section 5.5, below) Ancestry renders the donor’s ancestry as a donut chart with primary ancestry on the outer ring and secondary (sub-) ancestry on the inner ring (1000 Genomes-aligned), accompanied by tables of the underlying probabilities Genetic Risk Score shows T1D-GRS and T2D-GRS separately, with a dropdown that lets users switch between published scoring models (e.g. the 67-variant Luckett 2025, GRS2-Qu 2022, and Sharp 2021 for T1D; soft- and hard-clustering partitioned T2D scores under Smith 2024 / Suzuki 2024); each model shows the donor’s overall score plus a histogram of the donor’s position in the cohort distribution Dynamic Insulin Responses can be shown with or without normalisation to baseline Mitochondrial Function (oxygen consumption by Seahorse assay) can be shown with or without normalisation to DNA and baseline Prohormone Expression shows the donor’s C-peptide, proinsulin, and proinsulin:C-peptide ratio concentrations as values plus per-readout cohort histograms, with a selector to switch between the measured conditions Pancreas Lipid Extraction shows the donor’s total cholesterol (TC), triglycerides (TG), and free cholesterol (FC) (mg/g tissue) as values plus per-lipid cohort histograms 2.2.2 Phenotype View The Phenotype View lets users probe relationships among the 198 phenotype variables in the database (donor characteristics, isolation outcomes, secretion, mitochondrial function, perifusion, electrophysiology) without selecting any omics type. The page has a left-hand menu with three options: Overview — a scrollable heatmap of every donor against every phenotype variable, with cells coloured by each donor’s z-score against the population, so at a glance users can see where a donor sits across the full phenotype panel and spot clusters of donors that look similar across many measurements at once. A Visual Filter radio group lets users restrict the view to a specific Donor subset (chosen via the standard donor filter dialog, by characteristics, data availability, or a donor-ID upload) or to a Phenotype subset (chosen via the phenotype filter dialog); clicking Update re-renders the heatmap with the chosen filter, and once a donor subset is applied a Download Donor List button appears so the same selection can be re-used on the Data Download page. Use this view to spot outliers, compare a few donors against the whole cohort, or quickly check which phenotype variables are populated for a candidate analysis cohort. Correlation — an interactive chord diagram (and matching sortable table) of pairwise correlations among phenotype variables, kept only when they pass a user-set correlation-magnitude cutoff and a p-value cutoff. Users can restrict the analysis to a subset of donors or phenotypes and choose whether to include within-group correlations (off by default so the chord emphasises cross-group co-variation), then click Submit. The results panel has two tabs: Chord Diagram — clicking any ribbon opens the scatter plot of the underlying donor-level values for that pair, so users can immediately see whether the correlation is driven by a linear trend, by an outlier cluster, or by something more complex; the dialog supports CSV / SVG / PNG / PDF export. Correlation Table — every kept pair listed in a sortable, searchable table (green / red colour-coded for positive vs. negative r); clicking a row opens the same scatter dialog, and a Download CSV button exports the full list. Use this view to find phenotype variables that strongly co-vary, for example which insulin-secretion measures track each other or whether HbA1c correlates with electrophysiology readouts. Phenotype PCA — Probabilistic PCA (PPCA) on the donor × phenotype matrix, which (unlike standard PCA) tolerates the missing-value pattern typical of phenotype data. Users pick which two principal components to plot, a Color by variable to colour the donor points (two-tier selector with variable group → variable name, mirroring the Omics page; continuous variables get a gradient, categorical variables a discrete palette), and optionally one categorical Shape by variable so two metadata dimensions can be encoded on the same scatter at once. Two additional controls fine-tune the fit — Label outliers (how many donors to label automatically by distance from the centroid) and Min samples (minimum non-missing measurements per phenotype to be included). After clicking Submit, the results panel has two tabs: PCA Plot — donors in PC space, coloured / shaped per the chosen variables. Clicking any donor point opens a Donor Profile dialog showing the top 10 phenotype features where that donor deviates most from the population mean (blue = above, orange = below). Feature Loadings — every phenotype’s loading on the chosen PCs, ranked by importance (sum of |loadings| on the two PCs), with a Show top N selector and CSV export. Use this view to identify the major axes of phenotypic variation in the cohort, to spot outlier donors, and to see which phenotype variables drive that separation. 2.2.3 Cluster Explorer The Cluster Explorer presents a multi-omics-derived stratification of the cohort into five donor subgroups. The underlying clustering uses a multi-view Variational Autoencoder (VAE) trained on bulk RNA-seq and proteomics for the 387 donors with at least one of those omics; cluster labels are then extended to the 238 remaining donors using a LightGBM classifier trained on unbiased demographic and isolation features. The full pipeline (architecture, training, classifier extension, stability metrics) is described in chapter 5. The sidebar entry is “Cluster Explorer”; the page itself is titled Donor Explorer. Results layout. The page is organized into three top-level sections plus two modal dialogs: Donor UMAP Embedding + Donor Clusters (side by side). Left: a UMAP embedding of all donors, with a Color by selector (any phenotype) and a 2D / 3D dimension toggle. Network edges connect each donor to its 5 nearest neighbours in UMAP space; cross-cluster dashed edges link the closest donor pairs between clusters. Node size reflects connection degree. Right: a scrollable list of cluster cards with a Compare action that opens the per-cluster detail page. Donor Explorer. A donor-selection dropdown (currently 233 donors available for detailed inspection). Donor Details — selected donor’s age, BMI, HbA1c, diabetes diagnosis, sex, cluster assignment, and omics/predicted badge. Donor vs Cluster Comparison — per-phenotype Z-scores and percentiles for the donor against their cluster (showing only phenotypes deviating by > 0.8 SD). Similar Donors — ranked list of the most similar donors with similarity bars and cluster affiliation; for donors with omics this is cosine similarity in the VAE fused latent space, for predicted-only donors it is cluster probability agreement. Cluster-Phenotype Enrichments (heatmap). A heatmap of phenotype effect sizes (Cohen’s d) per cluster vs. rest, with tabs for Omics only, All donors, and (when available) a 3-view split. Clicking a cell opens an Enrichment Detail side panel with the cluster/phenotype names, effect size with directional arrow, p-value badge, cluster demographics, and related enrichments. The page also surfaces two modal dialogs on demand: an AI Insight Dialog (cluster analysis summary, phenotypes grouped by category, top 10 DE genes, AI interpretation) and a Similarity Breakdown Dialog for any selected donor pair (per-view breakdown bars for omics, most-similar-phenotype comparison table). Per-cluster detail page (reached from any cluster card on the right, route /cluster-detail/:id). Each cluster has its own page with these sections in order: Header — cluster name (e.g. C1), donor count, omics/predicted breakdown, and an AI Interpretation button (the AI summary appears inline when generated). Demographics — stat cards (Avg Age, Avg BMI, % Male, Avg HbA1c). Diagnosis Breakdown — badges with diagnosis label and count. Data Availability — badges listing omics modalities present in the cluster with their coverage (X / N donors). Phenotype Enrichment — paginated table of phenotype variables with the cluster’s effect direction, effect size, p-value, adjusted p-value, and per-cluster vs. outside means. Only held-out phenotype features are tested so that variables used as classifier input are excluded. Differential Expression — paginated table of RNA-seq and proteomics features with direction, log₂FC, t-statistic, raw p, and adjusted p. Filter buttons let users restrict by modality or to significant only. Enriched Pathways — paginated table from pre-ranked GSEA (KEGG 2021, Reactome 2022, GO MF 2023; pre-ranked GSEA with limma t-statistic ranking; raw p < 0.05 shown), with direction (↑ enriched in up-regulated, ↓ in down-regulated) and a clickable gene list. Explore Further — navigation cards linking to Omics Analysis, Multi-Omics (WGCNA), Metabolic Atlas, Knowledge Search, and Cluster Comparison. Cluster Comparison page (route /cluster-compare, reached either from a single cluster’s Explore Further → Cluster Comparison card or directly from any cluster card’s Compare action). Users pick two clusters to compare side by side and the page produces: A Phenotype Radar plot summarising how the two clusters differ across the platform’s phenotype variables. A Significant Phenotype Differences table listing the phenotypes that differ at p < 0.05 between the two clusters, with the effect direction (Enriched in C1 / Enriched in C2). A Pairwise DE Genes table listing the differentially expressed genes/proteins between the two clusters (p < 0.05), with filters for RNA-seq / Proteomics and a Source / Term filter for pathway-enrichment annotations. A toggle to restrict the comparison to Omics Only donors (those who contributed to the VAE) versus All Donors (including phenotype-classifier-extended donors), so users can see whether a comparison is driven by the omics evidence directly. 2.3 Search & Discovery The Search & Discovery block groups feature-centric and entity-centric exploration: ask a question about a gene/phenotype, or jump from one feature to all its associations. 2.3.1 Knowledge Search The Knowledge Search page lets users query any gene, protein, metabolite, reaction, or phenotype and returns integrated evidence about the entity. Users can either type a free-form natural-language question (e.g. “What does TCF7L2 do in beta cells?”) or enter a single symbol and the page resolves it to the right entity automatically. Results layout. The page opens with the search bar plus a row of example-question chips users can click as starter queries. After a query is run, the result renders as a tabbed card; the tab set depends on the kind of entity that was queried: Gene result — 4 tabs: Knowledge Graph Findings, Findings from Our Cohort, External Validation, AI Interpretation. Phenotype / Pathway / Reaction / Metabolite / Contaminant result — 3 tabs: Knowledge Graph Findings, Findings from Our Cohort, AI Interpretation (External Validation is folded into the KG / Cohort tabs for these entity types). Question result (free-form NL question) — 3 tabs: same trio plus an inline AI-suggestion banner above the tabs when the cohort can’t match the question directly, offering suggested cohort-mode chips, GWAS chips, and general/research-area chips to refine the question. What each tab shows (gene example; other entity types use the same conventions): Knowledge Graph Findings — disease associations (from PrimeKG), drug interactions (DrugBank/PrimeKG), biological pathways (Reactome/PrimeKG), Gene Ontology terms (biological process + molecular function), tissue expression with islet/pancreas highlighted, gene regulatory edges from Hetionet (with direction colour-coded), environmental exposures (CTD), and curated + on-demand literature evidence from PubMed/PubTator 3. Each item is clickable and drills into related entities. Findings from Our Cohort — the gene’s detection across HumanIslets omics datasets; direct phenotype correlations (with FDR/raw threshold radio + slider + Apply); multivariate predictors (with filter sliders for |importance|, model quality, and minimum number of methods); KEGG pathway annotations as a chip cloud; metabolic reactions from the GEM with high- and low-glucose flux; and the donor-subgroup (VAE cluster) context. External Validation (gene only) — database annotations (GWAS, NHGRI-EBI GWAS Catalog, local cohort QTL, TIGER islet eQTL, GTEx v8 eGene), a collapsible GWAS evidence tree (T2D / T1D / beta-cell trait groups with HuGE scores and a variants table), local cohort QTL details, TIGER islet eQTL details, and GTEx eGene tissue table. AI Interpretation — a generated natural-language summary of the above evidence. Explicitly framed as a hypothesis-generation aid for users to verify against the cited evidence and primary literature, not as a stand-alone authority. How the knowledge graph is built and what external sources feed each tab is documented in chapter 6 (Knowledge Graph). The page is currently labelled BETA while we collect feedback on the AI synthesis quality. 2.3.2 Feature View Instead of performing an ‘Omics Analysis’ focused on a particular omics type and metadata variable, users can search for a feature of interest and pull up ALL associations for that particular feature of interest, corrected for common covariates (see section 5.4, below). Searchable features include genes/proteins and also metadata variable. This allows rapid interrogation of precomputed results across all datasets. Searching for a gene/protein feature generates a table of results ranked by adjusted p-value. Searching for a metadata variable (e.g. BMI) generates a table of feature (gene/protein) results across all omics datasets, ranked by adjusted p-value. Clicking on rows and external links brings up categorical or scatterplots of the association, external pages for the feature of interest, or single-cell RNAseq expression for that feature. Because a large number of associations can be displayed, filtering of the metadata or omics type is enabled by clicking on the column title. 2.4 Data Download The Data Download page is a standalone top-level entry in the sidebar; it does not belong to one of the analytical categories above. 2.4.1 Data download All data at HumanIslets.com is available for download. Donors of interest can be filtered by Donor Characteristics (see section 3.1) and/or data availability. Advanced filters allow selection of donors of interest by any metadata available. Donors of interest can also be selected by uploading a list of donor IDs (for example, donor IDs obtained from a particular Omics Analysis). Data can be downloaded as Comma separated values (.csv), text (.txt), R object (.rds), or in Excel format (.xlsx) within a zip file. To perform data download: Under ‘Select Donors’ choose filtering parameters, upload a list of donor IDs Distribution of chosen donors compared with the entire dataset can be assessed by clicking on the icons at right Once donors of interest are selected/filtered, click ‘submit’ to choose those donors The total number of donors chosen will be displayed Under ‘Download Data’ Select the datasets of interest Select the data format The distribution of datasets selected, compared with the entire database, can be shown by clicking ‘Summary’ Click ‘Download’

Chapter 3 Overview of Primary Metadata 3.1 Donor Characteristics De-identified donor characteristics, including HLA-typing, collected at the time of organ procurement are provided in a de-identified manner to the ADI IsletCore team by the organ procurement organisations. Users should be aware that some information, such as diabetes diagnosis, may not be clinically validated at the time of organ retrieval. Whenever possible, HbA1c measurements are performed directly by the ADI IsletCore using blood samples sent with the organ, and donors indicated as type 1 diabetes (T1D) are validated as much as possible through genetic testing, autoantibody testing, and immunohistochemical analysis. While the Donor Metadata ‘diabetes diagnosis’ reflects the status indicated by the organ procurement organization, the Donor Metadata ‘corrected diabetes status’ indicates a classification based on the totality of information available to the ADI IsletCore (including ‘pre-diabetes’ defined as HbA1c between 5.7 and 6.5%, and ‘non-diagnosed’ donors with HbA1c>6.5% as type 2 diabetes (T2D)). Available donor characteristics metadata include: Age, Sex, Donation Type (NDD, DCD, MAID), BMI, HbA1c (%), HLA A2 (Yes/No), Diabetes diagnosis, and Corrected diabetes status. 3.2 Organ Characteristics and Processing Organ characteristic and processing metadata is recorded during the islet isolation by the ADI IsletCore lead technician. Cold ischemia time is calculated from the cross clamp time during organ procurement until the start of the isolation in Edmonton. Please note that some measures, such as ‘organ consistency’ are subjective. Available organ characteristics and processing metadata include: Cold Ischemic Time (h), Pancreas weight (g), Fatty infiltration (Yes/No), Organ consistency (Soft, Normal, Fibrotic, Inconsistent), Collagenase supplier (Roche, VitaCyte, Serva, Sigma), Colleganse type (Liberase, Clzyme, Gold, NB1, Sigma, Other, Recombinant), Digestion time (min). 3.3 Isolation Outcomes Islet purity, islet equivalents (IEQ), % trapped, and islet particle index (IPI) are determined by the ADI IsletCore lead technician. Full details can be found here or in our IsletCore welcome booklet. Details on determining Insulin and DNA samples as part of our quality control can be found here. Available isolation outcomes metadata include: Total islet equivalents (IEQ), Islet equivalents per pancreas weight (IEQ/g), Purity (%), Trapped (%), Islet particle index, Insulin content (ug), DNA content (ug), Insulin:DNA ratio, Insulin per IEQ (ng/IEQ). 3.4 Cell Type Proportions Cell proportions are estimated based on deconvolution of bulk islet proteomic data from hand-picked islets using canonical proteins as markers (see below). Estimated cell type proportions are shown as either the ‘non-endocrine cell proportion’ (of all cells) or as the proportion of the endocrine compartment only. Available cell type proportions metadata include: Non-endocrine cell proportion (computed), beta-cell proportion (of endocrine; computed), alpha-cell proportion (of endocrine; computed), delta-cell proportion (of endocrine; computed), and gamma-cell proportion (of endocrine; computed). 3.5 Cell Culture Outcomes Following isolation, islets are cultured prior to shipment and sample collection for assay. On the day of shipping/collection, islets are recounted to determine islet equivalents (IEQ) and islet particle index (IPI) after culture. Culture time is determined by the end of the isolation until approximately 9 am on a shipping/collection day. The % recovery is calculated as ’post-isolation IEQ/pre-shipment IEQ*100.’ Post-culture samples are collected for insulin and DNA as described. Available cell culture outcomes metadata include: Culture time (h), Total islet equivalents after culture (IEQ), Percent IEQ recovery after culture (%), Islet particle index after culture, Insulin content after culture, Insulin:DNA ratio after culture, Insulin per IEQ after culture (ng/IEQ). 3.6 Static Insulin Secretion On the day of sample collection (see section 2.5, above) islets are handpicked to 100% purity and cultured overnight at 37°C with 5% CO2. In triplicates, 15 hand-picked islets from each donor are pre-incubated at low glucose for 2 x 1 hours consecutively, and then sequentially treated with low glucose for 1 hour followed by high glucose 1 hour. Collected supernatant for low glucose, high glucose or insulin content are measured by ELISA (ALPCO STELLUX Chemiluminescence). For each donor, there are multiple glucose combinations that are used. Experimental details can be found here. Glucose pairs are: 1mM → 10mM glucose 1mM → 16.7mM glucose 2.8mM → 16.7mM glucose Outliers were handled in two ways. Level 1) A single donor-level measurement was computed as the median of the three replicate measures. Using the median instead of the mean reduced the impact of replicate-level outliers on the data. Level 2) The donor-level values were filtered to remove those that were more than 4 standard deviations away from the mean of all donors on a log10 scale. This removed values that were many orders of magnitude different from the population. In the Data Download tool, the data are provided at the replicate level (before outlier removal), while in-tool Omics Analysis is performed on data subjected to both Level 1 and Level 2 outlier removal. Available static insulin secretion metadata include: Culture time before experiment (days), Insulin content (pg/ml), Secretion at 1 mM glucose (pg/ml), Secretion at 2.8 mM glucose (pg/ml), Secretion at 10 mM glucose (pg/ml), Secretion at 16.7 mM glucose (pg/ml), Stimulation index (1->10 mM glucose), Stimulation index (1->16.7 mM glucose), Stimulation index (2.8->16.7 mM glucose). 3.7 Islet Oxygen Consumption (Seahorse assay) At the ADI IsletCore, after an overnight culture, islets are hand-picked, and 70 islets per well are assayed in triplicate for each donor. Islets are placed in the center of the well and islet capture screens are used to keep islets in place. Plate is incubated at 37oC for 1 hour without CO2, after which the plate is placed in the Xfe24 analyzer (Agilent). Basal measurements are at 2.8mM glucose, while stimulation is at 16.7mM glucose. 5uM oligomycin, 3uM FCCP and 5uM of rotenone and antimycin A are used as indicated. All solutions are made using DMEM supplemented with 1% FBS, sodium pyruvate and L-glutamine. After the run is complete, islets are collected and a DNA assay is done to normalize data. A detailed protocol is here. Available islet oxygen consumption (Seahorse assay) metadata include: Basal respiration, ATP-linked respiration, Proton leak, Max glucose response, Stimulated glucose response, Max respiration, and Spare capacity. 3.8 Dynamic Insulin Responses to Macronutrients After shipment to the University of British Columbia, islets are hand picked and cultured in RPMI for 24-72hrs before the experiment to allow recovery from shipping. 65 islets are loaded per chamber and pre-incubated for 1 hour. Islets are stimulated with 6 or 15mM glucose, 5mM Leucine or 0.75mM Oleic acid/0.75mM palmitic acid as indicated. Samples are stored at -20oC until analysis with RIA. Full details are described here. Available dynamic insulin responses to macronutrients metadata include: 3 mM Glucose baseline secretion (AUC), 15 mM Glucose-stimulated peak, 15 mM Glucose-stimulated secretion (AUC), 6 mM Glucose-stimulated secretion (AUC), 30 mM KCl-stimulated secretion, after glucose (AUC), 5 mM Leucine-stimulated peak secretion , 5 mM Leucine-stimulated secretion (AUC), 5 mM Leucine + 6 mM Glucose-stimulated secretion (AUC), 30 mM KCl-stimulated secretion, after leucine (AUC), 1.5 mM Oleate/palmitate-stimulated peak secretion, 1.5 mM Oleate/palmitate-stimulated secretion (AUC), 1.5 mM Oleate/palmitate _ 6 mM glucose-stimulated peak secretion, 30 mM KCl-stimulated secretion, after oleate palmitate (AUC). 3.9 Single-cell Function (electrophysiology) On the day of collection (see section 3.5 above) islets are hand-picked, dispersed to single cells and plated on 35 mm cell culture dishes as described here. 24-72 hours after plating, electrophysiology is performed by whole-cell patch-clamp using procedures and solutions outlined in publications here and here. For earlier donors, only ‘total exocytosis’ in response to a series of 10 membrane depolarizations from -70 to 0 mV is reported. For more recent donors (from R305 onward) an expanded panel of electrophysiological measures is reported that include measures of cell size, exocytosis, voltage-dependent Ca2+ channel activity, and voltage-dependent-Na+ channel activity. Cell types are identified either by immunostaining (insulin/glucagon) or by single-cell RNA sequencing after the experiment. In the Omics analysis, analyses can be performed on either alpha- or beta-cells, at 1, 5 or 10 mM glucose. Available single-cell function metadata include: Size (pF), Total exocytosis (fF/pF), Early (RRP) exocytosis (fF/pF), Late exocytosis (fF/pF), Calcium entry (pC/pF), Early calcium current amplitude (pA/pF), Late calcium current amplitude (pA/pF), Sodium current amplitude (pA/pF), Half inactivation sodium current (mV). 3.10 Prohormone Expression Current coverage: 126 donors. Prohormone expression measurements (insulin, glucagon and processing intermediates) are contributed by the lab of Dr. Bruce Verchere (University of British Columbia) and appear in the Donor View as a dedicated tab. [Author to double-check protocol details — methodology from a forthcoming Verchere-lab publication.] 3.11 Ancestry Current coverage: 404 donors. Donor DNA is extracted from acinar tissue and genotyped on the Illumina Omni2.5Exome BeadChip (~2.5M variants, V1.5 / V1.6 from October 2024 onward) at the Stanford Functional Genomics Core. Array-level QC requires minor allele frequency > 1%, SNP missing-genotype rate < 1%, donor missing-genotype rate < 3%, and reported-vs-genetic sex matching. The genotypes are then imputed against the NIH TOPMed reference panel via the BioData Catalyst TOPMed Imputation Server (variants called to GRCh38, aligned to the + strand, imputation quality R² > 0.3), yielding more than 41 million high-quality variants per donor. These imputed genotypes feed both the ancestry inference described here and the genetic risk scores in the next section. Genetic ancestry is inferred from the imputed genotypes using the Gloyn-lab GeneticAncestry pipeline (DOI:10.5281/zenodo.18157870). The pipeline first reduces the genotype matrix to its principal components, then classifies each donor with an ensemble of supervised models (Random Forest, SVM, XGBoost, LightGBM, and a multi-layer perceptron) trained on the 1000 Genomes high-coverage, unrelated sequencing samples (where ancestry is known). Two levels of ancestry assignment are produced and shown in the donor’s donut chart: Primary ancestry (super-population, 5 categories) on the outer ring of the donut, aligned with the 1000 Genomes primary populations. Secondary ancestry (sub-population, 26 categories) on the inner ring, providing the finer-grained breakdown within the primary population. See the 1000 Genomes population descriptions for the reference panels and population definitions, and the HIGI Pipeline methods PDF for the full Stanford pipeline overview. [Needs confirmation from the Gloyn lab.] 3.12 Genetic Risk Scores Current coverage: 404 donors. Polygenic risk scores are computed from the same TOPMed-imputed genotypes described in the previous section. All scores undergo a static min-max normalization based on the theoretical minimum and maximum genetic-risk contributions (i.e. no risk alleles up to all risk alleles), mapping each raw PRS onto a 0–1 scale. The displayed risk is then expressed as a percentile against the current IIDP donor data freeze (e.g. “Risk > 19% of IIDP donors”), and for donors of European ancestry it is additionally expressed against the UK Biobank reference population (e.g. “Risk > 25.27% of the European Population (UKBB)”). The Stanford implementation lives at github.com/sethsh7/PRSedm (DOI:10.5281/zenodo.17903985); the full pipeline methods document is the HIGI Pipeline PDF. T1D Genetic Risk Score (T1D-GRS). Multiple published T1D-GRS variants are computed for each donor and selectable from the model dropdown in the donor view: t1dgrs2-luckett25 — the 67-variant standardized score from Luckett et al. (2025, Diabetes Care, doi:10.2337/dc25-0142). t1dgrs2-qu22 — the original GRS2 PRS with 4 additional African-ancestry SNPs (from Onengut) proposed by Qu et al. (2022, Pediatric Diabetes) and used in eMERGE. t1dgrs2-sharp21 — the TOPMED-R2 version of GRS2 from Sharp et al. (2019, Diabetes Care, doi:10.2337/dc18-1785). Beyond the overall score, each donor’s T1D-GRS is decomposed into HLA Class 1, HLA Class 2, and Non-HLA contributions, and the donor’s DR3 / DR4 haplotype call is also reported (available via the IIDP Resource Data Repository). The trans-ancestry T1D-PRS is also downloadable from the RDR. T2D Genetic Risk Score (T2D-GRS). The genome-wide T2D-GRS is computed from the 1,232,226 variants of the multi-ancestry GWAS in Suzuki et al. (2024, Nature). Each donor is additionally given partitioned polygenic scores (pPS) that decompose their overall T2D risk into mechanistically distinct components, under a choice of two clustering frameworks: Soft clustering (Smith et al. 2024, Nature Medicine) — variants can contribute to multiple clusters with varying weights, allowing overlap and continuity across mechanisms. The clusters surfaced in the donor view include β-cell dysfunction (two), obesity, lipodystrophy (two), proinsulin, bilirubin, cholesterol, liver lipid, hyperinsulin, SHBGi-LpA, and Alp-negative. Hard clustering (Suzuki et al. 2024, Nature) — each variant is uniquely assigned to a single cluster, representing relatively discrete genetic mechanisms. The clusters include residual glycemic, body fat, metabolic syndrome, obesity, β-cell PI+, β-cell PI−, lipodystrophy, and liver-and-lipid metabolism. Both the ancestry and GRS pipelines are contributed by the lab of Dr. Anna Gloyn (Stanford); see the Contributors page. All data are released in versioned freezes: Version 2 is the current default (uses Suzuki 2024 for the T2D-GRS), while Version 1 is archived from the previous release (which used Mahajan et al. 2022 for the T2D-GRS and DiCorpo et al. 2022 for the partitioned scores). Both versions are accessible from the version dropdown in the donor view. [Needs confirmation from the Gloyn lab.] 3.13 Pancreas Lipid Extraction Current coverage: 149 donors. Lipid extraction is performed on whole pancreas samples (not isolated islets) for a subset of donors, contributed by the lab of Dr. Erin Mulvihill (University of Ottawa). Data appear in the Donor View as a dedicated tab. [Author to double-check protocol details — methodology from a forthcoming Mulvihill-lab publication.]

Chapter 4 Overview of Omics Data Types 4.1 Gene expression (bulk RNA-seq) Current coverage: 22,788 transcripts across 373 donors. On the day of sample collection, typically the shipment day (see section 3.5 above), 150 islets are hand picked and transferred to 1 ml of Trizol reagent. After vortexing, the sample is stored at -80°C until shipping or RNAseq at either Oxford or Stanford. At destination, RNA is extracted from Trizol as per manufacturer instructions and concentration and quality is assessed by NanoDrop. RNA integrity is further checked by Bioanalyzer (RNA 6000 chips) and 20 uL of RNA samples at 20 ng/uL minimum is submitted to for library prep and sequencing to Novogene, on NovaSeq 6000 PE150 illumina platform, at 20M paired end reads per sample (6 G of raw data). After quality control via FastqQC (v0.11.9), the raw reads were aligned to the human genome reference (GRCh38) using STAR (v2.7.9a) with the gene annotation downloaded from the ENSEMBL database (v110). Gene expression levels were counted using featureCounts (v2.0.3). Raw counts were normalized using the TPM method to adjust sequencing depth. Ensembl gene IDs were mapped to Entrez IDs and counts from any duplicated Entrez IDs were summed. Transcripts with over 80% zeros were filtered out. The RNAseq dataset contains seven batches, and significant batch effects were observed. Batch effects were corrected for using the Combat-Seq function in the sva R package (version 3.44.0), which preserves the count nature of the data. Counts were converted to log-counts-per-million (logCPM) using the relative log expression (RLE) method in the edgeR R package (version 3.38.4). Transcripts with logCPM variance in the lowest 20th percentile were filtered out. Since the sequencing depth was different across different batches, a substantial number of transcripts had zero counts in some batches and low but robust counts in other batches. These batch-related drop-out patterns led to large numbers of significant differentially expressed features that disappeared if individual batches were held out. To address this problem, counts with a value of zero or one were replaced with NAs, excluding these individual values from all downstream statistical analyses and leading to more stable differential expression analysis results. The raw RNA sequencing data has been deposited in the European Genome-phenome Archive (EGA) under study number EGAS00001007241. 4.2 Gene expression (Nanostring) Current coverage: 156 transcripts across 190 donors. On the day of sample collection (see section 3.5, above) 50 islets are hand-picked at the ADI IsletCore, washed with PBS, then lysed in 100ul of RNeasy Lysis Buffer (RLT, Qiagen) containing 1% beta-mercaptoethanol. Samples are stored at -80oC before running on Nanostring gene expression assay at BC Children Hospital (Vancouver). The assay was updated partway through data collection, where a small number of genes were removed and replaced with others, resulting in two different gene codesets. Briefly, 1ul cell lysate is used for hybridization with customized gene code sets that target 142 pancreatic islet genes and 6 reference genes. Hybridized complex is then loaded on cartridge and Nanostring nCounter to measure the target gene expression. The raw data is analyzed with nSolver4.0 software from Nanostring, producing a table of intensity values where larger values correspond to higher gene expression values. Full details are found here. Data Processing: Significant batch effects were present across codesets, and so transcripts measured in both Nanostring arrays were batch corrected using the Combat function from the sva R package (version 3.44.0). Gene expression intensities were normalized by sample median (each value divided by the median intensity of all genes for that donor) and log2-transformed. Given the much smaller number of transcripts compared to the RNAseq data, no abundance or variance filters were applied. 4.3 Protein expression (proteomics) Current coverage: 6,138 proteins across 134 donors. Human islets are shipped to the University of British Columbia, hand-picked and cultured in RPMI for 24-72hrs before the experiment to allow the islets to recover from shipping. After recovery, 300 islets are hand-picked, washed with PBS and the pellet is snap frozen and stored at -80oC. LC-MS/MS analysis is performed with n = 3 technical replicates, using a NanoElute UHPLC system (Bruker Daltonics) with Aurora Series Gen2 (CSI) analytical column coupled to a Trapped Ion Mobility – Time of Flight mass spectrometer (timsTOF Pro; Bruker Daltonics, Germany) operated in Data-Independent Acquisition - Parallel Accumulation- Serial Fragmentation (DIA-PASEF) mode. Details of lysis and analysis are found here. Protein abundances were filtered to remove any that had more than 50% missing values, median normalized, and log2 transformed. Missing values were imputed with the missForest method (random forest-based) using the imp4p R package (version 1.2.0). Uniprot IDs were converted to Entrez IDs, and rows with duplicate IDs were aggregated by taking the mean. Proteins with variance in the lowest 20th percentile were filtered out. Raw proteomics data has been deposited to ProteomExchange via MASSive under dataset number PXD045422. 4.4 Gene expression (pseudobulk RNA-seq) Current coverage: 106 donors with pseudobulk α-cell profiles, 65 donors with pseudobulk β-cell profiles (plus smaller numbers for δ and PP cells). The single-cell counts from patch-seq data (section 3.5, below) were summarized at the donor by separating counts by cell type (alpha and beta). Only cells from donors with at least five cells were retained. Pseudobulk profiles were created by summing counts from all cells derived from the same donor. Ensembl gene IDs were mapped to Entrez IDs and counts from any duplicated Entrez IDs were summed. Each matrix was filtered to remove transcripts with greater than 80% zeros. Counts were converted to logCPM values using the TMM method from the edgeR R package (version 3.38.4). Due to the large variations in sequencing depth across batches, individual transcripts that had zero or one count were replaced with NA values. 4.5 Single-cell gene expression (patch-seq) The collection and analysis of pancreas patch-seq data is described here and here. Reads from FASTQ files were trimmed using TrimGalore version 0.6.5 with auto-detection of adapter sequence, then aligned and counted using STAR version 2.7.9a to the GRCh38 human genome (Ensembl release 104). Cell count matrices were loaded into R and a Seurat object was generated using the Seurat R package (version 4.3.0). Only cells with patch-clamp data were included; data for additional cells are available from the listed accession numbers below. Cell types were identified by projection, using the MapQuery command from the R package Seurat, onto a large set of human islet single cell RNAseq data generated from publically available data (see below). Cells were filtered to remove any that were not alpha or beta cells, had fewer than 500 total counts across all transcripts, had fewer than 300 unique transcripts with non-zero count values, or had greater than 20% mitochondrial DNA. Ensembl gene IDs were mapped to Entrez IDs and counts from any duplicated Entrez IDs were summed. Counts were separated by cell type (alpha and beta) and glucose concentration (1, 5, and 10mM), resulting in six different matrices. Each matrix was filtered to remove transcripts with greater than 80% zeros. Counts were converted to logCPM values using the trimmed mean of M-values (TMM) method from the edgeR R package (version 3.38.4). TMM has been shown to perform better for single-cell RNAseq data compared to the RLE method because it is robust to the high drop-out rate. Individual transcripts that had zero counts were replaced with NA. Raw sequencing reads are available in the NCBI Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) and Sequence Read Archive (SRA) under accession numbers GSE124742 and GSE164875 and GSE270484 at PancDB (https://hpap.pmacs.upenn.edu/). 4.6 Single-cell RNA-seq Reads from FASTQ files were trimmed using TrimGalore version 0.6.5 with auto-detection of adapter sequence, then aligned to the GRCh38 human genome, Ensembl release 104, and counted using STAR version 2.7.9a except for files from GSE84133, which were aligned and counted using the inDrops protocol; files from GSE81076 and GSE85241, which were aligned and counted using the R package scruff (version 1.10.1) with Rsubread (version 1.22.2); and files from GSE269204, which were aligned and counted with Cell Ranger (10X Genomics Cell Ranger v6.1.2). Cell count matrices were loaded into R and objects generated using the R package Seurat (version 4.3.0), restricted to transcripts detected in more than 10 cells and cells with between 200 and 12,500 unique transcripts with non-zero count values; cells with percent mitochondrial transcripts in the top 25% for each dataset were also dropped. Transcript names were maintained as Ensembl identifiers to preserve the greatest number of unique transcripts. After combining all donors, transcripts expressed in fewer than 0.1% of cells were filtered. Donor HPAP-027 was dropped from the data set because there were very few cells (32 cells). The reciprocal PCA integration with references (HPAP-037, HPAP-040; HPAP-045, HPAP-050, HPAP-052, and HPAP-072) workflow from the R package Seurat (version 4.3.0) was followed, with k.anchor = 5, then FindNeighbors and FindCluster (resolution = 1.2). Cell types were first manually annotated based on the unambiguous, high level expression of marker genes in distinct clusters in UMAP space, except cytotoxic T cells, which did not form a distinct cluster but had restricted expression of TRAC. The remaining ambiguous cells were annotated using the MapQuery function by projecting unknown cells onto the manually annotated cells. Although these data include cells from donors with type 1 diabetes and type 2 diabetes, only cells from donors with no diabetes (ND) are visualised in the web tool for cell-type localization of called features, and are not downloadable . Data are from the NCBI Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) and Sequence Read Archive (SRA) under accession numbers GSE81076, GSE81547, GSE81608, GSE83139, GSE84133, GSE85241, GSE86469, GSE154126; from the European Molecular Biology Laboratory European Biology Institute (EMBL-EBI) under accession number E-MTAB-5061; from the Human Pancreas Analysis Program at PancDB up to donor HPAP-109, and from unpublished data (GSE269204). 4.7 Metabolomics Current coverage: 215 annotated metabolite features across 43 donors, measured at low (2.8 mM) and high (16.7 mM) glucose, with ratio also reported. Metabolomics samples are collected on islets handled at the ADI IsletCore under the same two glucose conditions used elsewhere in the platform — low glucose (LG, 2.8 mM) and high glucose (HG, 16.7 mM) — so that metabolite intensities can be compared at fasting-like versus stimulation-like conditions and an HG/LG ratio can be reported alongside each absolute value. Samples are analysed by liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry. Raw mzML spectra are acquired across two batches (B1 and B2) in both positive- and negative-ion modes. Peak picking is performed with Asari (xialab’s open-source MS preprocessor), producing per-mode preferred feature tables and a feature-level annotation file. Feature tables are then processed in MetaboAnalyst (xialab’s metabolomics platform; see https://www.metaboanalyst.ca/). Post-processing of the MetaboAnalyst output is implemented in a custom R pipeline: Blank filtering — features that are not appreciably elevated over process blanks are dropped. Detection (QC) filtering — features whose intensity is unstable across pooled QC injections are dropped. Normalization — median normalisation (each sample divided by its median feature intensity) followed by log transform. Batch correction — the production pipeline uses ComBat (parametric empirical Bayes, sva R package), with donor diabetes status included as a protected covariate so the inter-batch correction preserves biological T2D-versus-no-diabetes signal. Three alternatives were benchmarked during method selection — WaveICA 2.0 (wavelet + ICA, no batch labels required), QC-RLSC (QC-based LOESS signal correction), and median-only — and ComBat-with-diagnosis was selected on the basis of PCA separation and downstream replicability. MS2 annotation — once corrected, the feature tables are merged with MS2-level annotations (HMDB IDs, main class, sub class) to produce the final 215 annotated metabolites surfaced in the platform. Positive- and negative-mode results are combined into a single donor × metabolite matrix at low glucose, high glucose, and as the HG/LG log-ratio. 4.8 Environmental Contaminants Current coverage: 234 contaminant features across 34 donors. Some entries gated to authorized users pending publication. Environmental chemical contaminants are quantified in islet and tissue samples by the lab of Dr. Jenny Bruin (Carleton University). Because contaminant data are typically zero-inflated and non-normal, in-tool analyses use Kendall rank correlation (partial Kendall when covariates are supplied) rather than the moderated-t approach used for transcriptomics/proteomics; see chapter 5 (Kendall rank correlation) for details. The detailed measurement and quality-control protocol is described in Hoyeck et al. (2026) Nature Communications, doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71847-5. [Author to double-check this summary against the Hoyeck 2026 paper for accuracy.] 4.9 Metabolic Flux Current coverage: 2,771 reactions at high glucose (2,775 at low glucose; 2,765 in ratio form) across 375 donors. Donor-specific genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs) are built from the Human-GEM v1.19.0 reconstruction and loaded into COBRApy from per-donor MATLAB-format model files. ATP hydrolysis (Human-GEM reaction MAR03964) is set as the cellular objective. The platform simulates two glucose conditions and two media compositions: Low glucose (LG): approximately 2.8 mM (the glucose exchange reaction is bounded at −2.0 flux units, matching the GSIS / perifusion / metabolomics LG condition). High glucose (HG): approximately 16.7 mM (glucose exchange bounded at −10.0 flux units, matching the platform’s HG stimulation condition). Both conditions are simulated under glucose-only media and under glucose plus amino-acid supplementation (with fixed uptake rates for glutamine, alanine, serine, etc.); the amino-acid scenario opens alternative carbon entry into the TCA cycle. For each donor × condition, Flux Variability Analysis (FVA) is run on the entire reaction network at a 90% optimality fraction; the platform stores the FVA minimum, maximum, centre, and span per reaction. OptGP flux sampling (5,000 samples, thinning = 100) is run on top of FVA to capture the achievable flux distribution; from the samples the pipeline records the mean, median, standard deviation, and 25/75% quartiles per reaction. Per-reaction flux values are signed-log2 transformed and z-scored within each donor before being loaded into the analysis tables so the downstream limma model sees comparable distributions across reactions. The HG/LG ratio table stores the per-reaction log-ratio between high glucose and low glucose conditions, capturing each donor’s glucose responsiveness at the flux level.
